1.note: The son of Enosh, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God — the Chronicler begins the entire story of Israel with Adam (v1), anchoring the Davidic covenant in universal human origins. Luke's genealogy of Jesus does the same in reverse: tracing back through the patriarchs and Israel's history to Adam and then to God. Both texts signal that the covenant story is not tribal but cosmic in scope; the Messiah who descends from David ultimately descends from humanity's first parent.

The first man Adam became a living being; the last Adam became a life-giving spirit — Paul's Adam-Christ typology in 1 Cor 15 depends on the same theological weight that Chronicles places on Adam as the starting point. By opening the entire genealogy at Adam rather than Abraham, the Chronicler signals that the covenant's end-goal is something as universal as Adam: a renewed humanity, not merely a restored nation.

28.note: It is written that Abraham had two sons, one from a slave woman and one from a free woman... the one born of the slave was born according to the flesh, but the one born of the free woman was born through promise — 1 Chr 1:28 names Isaac and Ishmael side by side as Abraham's sons before narrowing to the covenant line. Paul's allegory in Gal 4 takes this same two-sons structure and makes explicit the theological distinction implicit in the genealogy's selective narrowing: one son represents the flesh, the other the promise.

34.note: It was said: the older will serve the younger. As it is written: Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated — v34 names the sons of Isaac as Esau and Israel (in that birth-order), then immediately proceeds to spend twenty verses on Esau's descendants (v35-54) before the rest of Chronicles focuses entirely on Israel. Paul in Romans 9 expounds the election-theology embedded in this genealogical structure: the narrowing that Chronicles performs (Esau's list and then Israel's story) enacts the divine preference for Jacob. The genealogy is not merely historical; it is an argument about grace.

13.note: I will be to him a father and he shall be to me a son — Chronicles repeats the Davidic covenant promise of 2 Sam 7:14; Hebrews cites it to establish Christ's superiority to angels as the eternal Son who holds the Davidic throne

11.note: Yours O LORD is the greatness and the power and the glory and the victory and the majesty — David's prayer at the temple offering is the OT source behind the doxology appended to the Lord's Prayer in Matthew 6:13: For yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory forever

4.note: Judah was the father of Perez and Zerah by Tamar — Matthew's genealogy of Jesus explicitly names Tamar (Matt 1:3), following the Chronicles genealogy's own inclusion of her alongside Perez. The Chronicler's inclusion of Tamar in the patrilineal list signals that the covenant line runs through unexpected vessels; Matthew intensifies this pattern by naming four women in the genealogy, of whom Tamar is the first.

15.note: A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse — Isaiah's messianic oracle uses Jesse rather than David as the root image, deliberately reaching past the dynastic line to its origin. The Chronicles genealogy's explicit tracing of the line Jesse → David (v13-15) is the scriptural basis for this Isaianic image. When the dynasty is cut down like a tree, the promise does not die; the Messianic shoot comes from the same root from which David himself came.

17.note: After the deportation to Babylon: Jeconiah was the father of Shealtiel, and Shealtiel the father of Zerubbabel — Matthew's post-exilic genealogical section follows 1 Chr 3:17-19 precisely (Jeconiah → Shealtiel → Zerubbabel). The significance is that the Davidic line did not end at the exile; Chronicles traces it carefully through the years of Babylonian captivity. Matthew's genealogy depends on Chronicles' testimony that the covenant line survived.

On that day, says the LORD of hosts, I will take you, O Zerubbabel my servant, son of Shealtiel, and make you like a signet ring, for I have chosen you — the oracle to Zerubbabel in Haggai reverses Jer 22:24 (where Jehoiachin/Coniah's signet ring was removed as a sign of curse). Chronicles 3:17-19's genealogy gives this oracle its messianic weight: Zerubbabel is the heir of the cursed Jeconiah, yet YHWH reverses the curse and makes Zerubbabel the signet. The post-exilic line is under restoration.

1.note: The sons of Judah: Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal — the Chronicler's Judah genealogy is part of the ancestral chain Matthew traces from Abraham to Jesus. Matthew 1:3 names Perez and Hezron as direct ancestors of Christ; the 1 Chronicles 4 Judah genealogy provides the tribal context that roots Jesus's Davidic lineage within the tribe of Judah.

9.note: Jabez was more honoured than his brothers — the Jabez episode is embedded in an undifferentiated genealogical list precisely because of his exemplary prayer. James 5:16 reflects the same conviction: 'the prayer of a righteous person is powerful and effective.' Jabez's single recorded prayer, offered in covenant faith, demonstrates that God's blessing is available to those who call on him regardless of circumstances.

10.note: Jabez called on the God of Israel: 'Oh that you would bless me and enlarge my territory! Let your hand be with me, and keep me from evil' — God's invitation in Jer 33:3 ('call to me and I will answer you and tell you great and hidden things you do not know') is the prophetic counterpart to the Jabez episode: both ground covenant prayer in the divine character as the answerer of those who call in faith.

1.note: Reuben's birthright was given to the sons of Joseph — the double-portion went to Joseph (Ephraim and Manasseh), but the tribal leadership remained with Judah. This implements the pattern Gen 49:10 announced: 'the scepter shall not depart from Judah.' Joseph received the material inheritance; Judah retained the royal prerogative.

2.note: The ruler came from Judah — the Chronicler explicitly states that the nagiyd (leader/ruler) comes from Judah. Hebrews 7:14 applies this to Christ: 'it is evident that our Lord was descended from Judah, and in connection with that tribe Moses said nothing about priests.' The tribe-of-Judah identification is the Chronicler's genealogical anticipation of what Hebrews calls evident about Jesus.

The ruler came from him [Judah] — the elder's declaration 'the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has triumphed' (Rev 5:5) draws on the same tribal identification that 1 Chr 5:2 announces. The Chronicler's statement that the ruling prerogative belongs to Judah is the OT genealogical foundation for Revelation's messianic title.

20.note: They were helped because they cried to God during the battle, and he answered them because they trusted in him — the Transjordanian tribes' battle victory through prayer and trust is an instance of the pattern Hebrews 11:33-34 celebrates: those who through faith 'became mighty in war, put foreign armies to flight.' The 1 Chr 5 battle is one of the OT episodes behind Hebrews' faith-catalog.

25.note: They were unfaithful to the God of their fathers and prostituted themselves to the gods of the peoples of the land — the Chronicler's theological verdict on the Transjordanian tribes mirrors the pattern Paul describes in Romans 1: they knew God but turned to idols, and the divine giving-over followed. The 1 Chr 5:25-26 sequence (unfaithfulness → exile) is the covenant-community case of the universal pattern Paul generalizes.

26.note: The God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Tilgath-pilneser king of Assyria, and he took them into exile — the divine sovereignty over pagan empires as instruments of covenant discipline is the OT framework for Jesus's prediction in Luke 21:24 that Jerusalem would 'be trampled on by the Gentiles until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled.' The Assyrian exile of the Transjordanian tribes is an early instance of this pattern.
